280:Correlations between the pharmacokinetic parameters became significantly stronger when the patients were stratified based on the *NAT2* acetylator status; the majority of the observed correlation coefficients were statistically significant, and clear separation between *NAT2* groups was observed ([Figures 2C, D, G, H](#F2)). On the other hand, stratification of the patients based on the *GSTM1* genotype did not improve the correlation values for the pharmacokinetic parameters, and clear group separation was not observed ([Supplementary Figure S4](#s12)).
282:In the whole cohort, significant correlation was observed between both INH metabolites INA (AUC_0–6h_) and AcINH (AUC_0–6h_), as well as between AcINH/INH and INA/INH ratios ([Figures 3A, D](#F3)). Again, group separation was observed for *NAT2* SA and IA acetylators, but not for the *GSTM1* genotypes ([Figures 3B, C, E, F](#F3)).
290:XY plots showing correlation analysis results for isoniazid metabolites and metabolic ratios in blood plasma of patients with tuberculosis. (A) Acetylisoniazid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL) versus isonicotinic acid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL) in the whole study cohort. (B) Acetylisoniazid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL) versus isonicotinic acid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL), patient samples were stratified based on NAT2 SA and IA acetylator status. (C) Acetylisoniazid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL) versus isonicotinic acid (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL), patient samples were stratified based on GSTM1 genotypes. (D) Correlation between INA/INH and AcINH/INH ratios in the whole study cohort. (E) Correlation between INA/INH and AcINH/INH ratios, patient samples were stratified based on NAT2 SA and IA acetylator status. (F) Correlation between INA/INH and AcINH/INH ratios, patient samples were stratified based on GSTM1 genotypes. The solid lines represent the Pearson correlation coefficient. AcINH and INA (AUC0–6h µg·h/mL) values were log-trans formed before running the tests. Spearman correlation was used for non-normally distributed variables (i.e., AcINH/INH and INA/INH ratios). Shaded areas represent the 95% confidence interval of the observed concentration mean values. Dots represent the observed data for a single patient. INH, isoniazid; AcINH, acetylisoniazid; INA, izonicotinic acid; AUC, area under the concentration-time curve; NAT2, N-acetyltransferase 2; SA, slow acetylator; IA, intermediate acetylator; GSTM1, glutathione S-transferase mu 1 class.
292:After adjustment by sex, age and INH dose, the mean and median values of the pharmacokinetics parameters differed significantly between *NAT2* SA and IA groups, except for INA AUC_0–6h_ ([Table 4](#T4)). More specifically, INH AUC_0–6h_ and INH C_max_ values were significantly higher, while AcINH AUC_0–6h_ values were significantly lower in *NAT2* slow acetylators. Also, median values of both AcINH/INH and INA/INH ratios were significantly lower in the *NAT2* SA group. None of pharmacokinetic parameters differed significantly between *GSTM1*-plus and -null genotypes.
300:Based on the univariate analysis, there were no significant associations between treatment outcome and any of genetic factors studied ([Table 5](#T5)). DIH occurred only in the patients with NAT2 SA phenotype (*N* = 4); however, statistically significant difference was not observed between NAT2 SA and IA groups ([Table 5](#T5)). A trend of association between NAT2 phenotypes and tSCC was detected, with SA having 80% lower odds of tSCC within 60 days in comparison to IA (OR = 0.2; 95% CI, 0.03–1.22); however, statistical significance was not reached (*p* = 0.069) ([Table 5](#T5)).
307:| NAT2 phenotype | SA | 23/34 (67.7) | 18/23 (78.3) | 3/23 (13.0) | 8/18 (44.4) | 10/18 (55.6) | 4/23 (17.4) | 19/23 (82.6) |
345:Table 5 Caption: Treatment outcome and hepatotoxicity in patients with TB (n = 34) stratified by NAT2 phenotype, GSTM1 genotype, and CYP2E1 SNP (rs) variants.
349:Cumulative probabilities of the sputum culture conversion event were estimated as the complements of Kaplan-Meier curves ([Figure 4](#F4)). According to the log-rank test, there was no significant difference in tSCC between the genotypes of either NAT2 (*p* = 0.16), or *GSTM1* (*p* = 0.33). Based on the *GSTM1* stratification, the median treatment times were 64 (95% CI, 42–85) and 66 (95% CI, 46–197) days for null vs. plus genotype, respectively. The mean tSCC, restricted at time 60 days, were 46.7 (95% CI, 38.9–54.5) vs. 50 (95% CI, 43.6–56.5) days for null vs. plus genotype, respectively; the difference between plus and null groups was 3.4 days (95% CI, 13.5–6.7), *p* = 0.51. Based on the NAT2 phenotype stratification, the median treatment times were 46 (95% CI, 27–197) and 71.5 (95% CI, 57–112) days for NAT2 IA and SA individuals, respectively. The mean tSCC, restricted at time 60 days, were 39.6 (95% CI, 29.3–49.9) vs. 52.4 (95% CI, 47.2–57.5) days for NAT2 IA and SA individuals, respectively; the difference between NAT2 IA and SA groups was 12.7 days (95% CI, 1.2–24.3), *p* = 0.03 ([Figure 4](#F4)).
357:Kaplan-Meier curves of cumulative probability of the sputum culture conversion time along with the table of cumulative number of events. (A) Data were stratified based on GSTM1 genotype. (B) Data were stratified based on NAT2 SA and IA acetylator status. The log-rank test was used for comparison of the curves, the corresponding p-values are displayed. Median treatment times in subgroups are indicated by dashed lines. NAT2, N-acetyltransferase 2; SA, slow acetylator; IA, intermediate acetylator; GSTM1, glutathione S-transferase mu 1 class.
359:However, in the Cox regression model for the whole study cohort (*n* = 34) incorporating *GSTM1* genotype, NAT2 phenotype, CYP2E1*6 (T > A) allelic variant (rs6413432), and smear microscopy, none of the predictors contributed significantly on HR for the respond to treatment (i.e., tSCC); global log-rank *p*-value of the model was *p* = 0.081 and concordance index was 0.64 ([Figure 1C](#F1)).
363:This is the prospective study aimed to decipher the impact of pharmacokinetic, pharmacogenetic and patient-related factors on the response to TB treatment, TB treatment outcome and the risk of DIH, and to investigate possible links between these variables. For this task, along with the array of patient- and disease-related factors, we evaluated six pharmacokinetic parameters of the parent drug INH and its two metabolites, AcINH and INA, for more accurate assessments of the data obtained ([Akkerman et al., 2014](#B2); [Sturkenboom et al., 2015](#B53)). In our study, a high TB treatment success rate was achieved (94.1%). However, we did not observe any associations between patient-related factors with either DIH or treatment outcome. Molla and colleagues have reported that males were significantly less likely to develop hepatotoxicity than females ([Molla et al., 2021](#B34)); in our study similar trend was observed, however, statistical significance was not reached (*p* = 0.071). Smoking status was considered to be a baseline risk factor associated with negative impact on the TB treatment success, delaying conversion of the sputum culture, and extending the time of TB treatment ([Silva et al., 2018](#B47); [World Health Organization, 2022](#B60)). Here, smoking was highly associated with positive sputum microscopy results confirming the negative smoking impact. However, both smokers and patients with positive sputum microscopy results were more likely to achieve treatment outcome “cured” in comparison to the “treatment completed” or “lost to follow up” outcomes (*p* < 0.05). This result could be related to the fact that patients with positive sputum microscopy result were treated in the hospital until the negative sputum microscopy was confirmed, rather than received ambulatory treatment for TB, thus achieving better treatment compliance. These findings could have some implications for TB eradication programmes. NAT2 enzyme plays a key role in biotransformation of xenobiotics including first-line anti-TB drug INH ([Mthiyane et al., 2020](#B35)). The *NAT2* gene is highly polymorphic, and more than 65 variants containing one or more SNPs in the 870 bp long coding region have previously been reported ([Hein and Doll, 2012](#B18)); in turn, the state of NAT2 activity in an individual depends on combinations of rapid and slow alleles ([Hein and Doll, 2012](#B18); [Mthiyane et al., 2020](#B35)). Worldwide, significant differences in the distribution and frequency of NAT2 alleles between ethnic populations exist; the overall proportions of the acetylator phenotypes in our study cohort was 68% for SA and 32% for IA carriers, similar to those reported in Caucasian populations in Europe and the United States ([Sabbagh et al., 2011](#B42); [Bisso-Machado et al., 2016](#B7)).
365:Data from our study confirmed previous reports reviewed in ([Hong et al., 2020](#B19)) showing that INH and its metabolites AcINH and INA blood plasma levels were significantly different between the NAT2 slow and intermediate acetylators: INH AUC_0–6h_ and INH C_max_ were significantly higher, and AUC_0–6h_ values for both metabolites were significantly lower in the SA group in comparison to the IA group; consequently, the median AcINH/INH and INA/INH ratio values were also significantly lower in the SA group. Also, significant NAT2 inter-phenotypic differences in INH and metabolites correlation patterns were detected.
367:According to the proposed INH metabolism pathway, INH, Hz and AcHz are metabolized by NAT2 enzyme and thus are subjects to the same acetylation polymorphism ([Balhara et al., 2021](#B5)). Three metabolites have been proposed to be responsible for INH-induced liver injury, AcHz, Hz and a metabolite resulting from the bioactivation of INH itself ([Metushi et al., 2011](#B33)). In our study, AcINH AUC_0–6h_ values were significantly lower in patients with DIH suggesting impaired INH acetylation in these cases; in addition, slow acetylation of toxicologically active AcHz to inactive diAcHz could be expected.The frequency of DIH observed in our cohort (11.76%), as well as the association of a slow acetylation profile with DIH, are consistent with the data reported previously ([Sistanizad et al., 2011](#B49); [Perwitasari et al., 2018](#B38); [Khan et al., 2019](#B25)). In our small cohort, statistical significance was reached for this INH no other INH pharmacogenetic parameters reached statistical significance, thus these results indicated that AcINH plasma level could serve as a biomarker for DIH.GSTs are important phase II detoxification isozymes that catalyse the conjugation of reduced form of glutathione to toxic metabolites including in the INH metabolic pathway, thereby reducing their reactivity toward cellular macromolecules ([Sodhi et al., 1996](#B50); [Eaton and Bammler, 1999](#B13)). GST genetic polymorphisms, especially the variants of *GSTM1* gene, have been extensively studied, and the absence of *GSTM1* activity due to null mutation was associated with accumulation of toxic intermediates leading to DIH in some studies ([Wang et al., 2016](#B57)). The proportion of the *GSTM1*-null genotype in our Caucasian cohort was 59%; however, we did not observe any effects of *GSTM1* genotype on DIH, TB treatment outcome, or any of the six INH pharmacokinetic parameters studied.
369:CYP2E1 is another enzyme involved in the Phase I biotransformation reactions of INH ([Cheng et al., 2013](#B10)). Previous studies on the pharmacogenetic variability of *CYP2E1* have resulted in evidence-based clinical annotations of specific genetic polymorphisms and corresponding allelic variants with consequent different phenotypes in regulatory pathways that effect the pharmacokinetics and treatment response ([https://cpicpgx.org/guidelines](https://cpicpgx.org/guidelines)), but controversial associations of *CYP2E1* genotype with risk of INH-related hepatotoxicity were reported ([Lei et al., 2021](#B29)). In our study, statistically significant associations between *CYP2E1**6 (T > A) allelic variant (rs6413432) and three INH pharmacokinetic parameters, i.e., increase in INH AUC_0–6h_, and decrease in both AcINH/INH and INA/INH ratios, were detected; however, no direct proofs for contribution in either DIH or treatment outcome were observed. None of other detected SNPs, including two allelic variants *CYP2E1**4 (rs6413419) and *CYP2E1**7A (rs2070673) which were previously associated with potential effects on CYP2E1 enzymatic activity, showed any statistically significant associations.
371:*CYP2E1**6 allelic variant, recognized by *Dra* I restriction enzyme, is located in intron 6 of the gene ([Fang et al., 2017](#B16)). Previously, it was suggested that this polymorphism is associated with the *CYP2E1* gene expression at mRNA level ([Uematsu et al., 1994](#B55)). Our data indicate that *CYP2E1**6 was related to the decreased amounts of AcINH and INA; however, the mechanism of interaction between *CYP2E1* genetic polymorphisms and INH plasma levels is unclear. Clinical studies have reported the interactions of INH with CY2E1 substrates including observations of interphenotypic difference for INH-related inhibition of *CYP2E1* activity, which was more extensive in slow acetylators ([O’Shea et al., 1997](#B36)). However, *in vitro* studies did not observe any significant inhibition of CYP2E1 by INH or its metabolites ([Balhara et al., 2021](#B5)). Also, treatment of drug-susceptible TB involves several drugs, including RIF, which is a CYP2E1 inducer, thus drug-drug interactions could potentially interfere with INH metabolism or manifestation of INH-related adverse drug reactions through increased production of reactive metabolites, especially in slow acetylators ([Shen et al., 2008](#B46); [Yamada et al., 2009](#B63)). Additional studies are required to determine the possible functional implications of the *CYP2E1* SNVs by assessing the drug response and clinical manifestations of the identified variants.
373:Another objective of our study was to determine the possible relationship between INH pharmacokinetic data and TB treatment outcome. First, it should be noted that, in our study, INH C_max_ at 2 h time point was below the therapeutic reference range in the majority of the patients (64.7%). Considering the overall good treatment success rates, our findings support the opinion that the current therapeutic ranges are probably set too high ([Perumal et al., 2020](#B37)). Further, our results showed that INA/INH ratios were significantly higher in patients with SCC time less than 60 days. This assertion was further supported by the findings showing that a one unit increase of the standardized AcINH/INH ratio value gave 58% probability to achieve SCC first. Thus, it could be concluded, that for a patient to have a probability of healing first, the metabolic rate of the standard dose INH should be sufficiently high, while non-efficient INH metabolic rate is not favourable. Also, quite surprisingly, our results from the time-to-event analysis showed that intermediate acetylators recovered more rapidly than slow acetylators; however, the results of univariate analysis did not reach statistically significant difference between NAT2 phenotypes and tSCC. Somewhere controversially, several studies have shown that low INH C_max_ and/or AUC values in rapid acetylators were associated with longer tSCC and a higher likelihood of failure or relapse; however, other studies, and also this study, found no association between the INH concentrations and treatment outcomes ([Sekaggya-Wiltshire, von Braun, et al., 2018](#B45)). INH plasma half-life is approximately 70 min in fast and 2–5 h in slow acetylators ([Goodman et al., 2006](#B17)), thus INH C_max_ measurements at a single 2 h time point may not fully reflect INH exposure for all patients. Thus, in our study, plasma concentrations of AcINH and INA were measured, and INH metabolic ratio values were used in addition to INH C_max_ and AUC values, which could provide more accurate assessments of the data.

I now have a thorough understanding of all 13 papers. Let me compile the final summary.

---

Here is the detailed summary for each file:

---

## File-by-File Summary

---

### 1. PMC10532840.md
**Title:** TNFα rs1800629 Polymorphism and Response to Anti-TNFα Treatment in Behcet Syndrome

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses entirely on the TNFα rs1800629 (-308G>A) SNP and its association with response to anti-TNFα therapy in Behcet syndrome (an auto-inflammatory vasculitis). NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere in the paper. No acetylator phenotype, NAT2 gene variants, or related content is discussed.

---

### 2. PMC10995391.md
**Title:** Effect of NAT2, GSTM1 and CYP2E1 genetic polymorphisms on plasma concentration of isoniazid and its metabolites in patients with tuberculosis, and the assessment of exposure-response relationships

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** YES — Implicitly via phenotype-based classification using the 7-SNP NAT2 signature panel and the NAT2 allele database (nat.mbg.duth.gr). The paper classifies patients as slow acetylators (SA, carrying two slow NAT2 alleles) and intermediate acetylators (IA, one rapid and one slow allele), based on the conventional 7-SNP genotyping method. NAT2*4 is the reference rapid allele; slow alleles include NAT2*5, *6, and *7. While the paper does not enumerate individual patients' specific star allele pairs, it directly invokes the allele classification system that includes NAT2*4, *5, *6, and *7.

**Drugs mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Isoniazid (INH) — primary drug studied; INH pharmacokinetics (AUC, Cmax, half-life) are strongly NAT2 genotype-dependent
- Rifampicin (RIF) — mentioned as a co-prescribed first-line anti-TB drug and CYP2E1 inducer that may interact with INH metabolism in slow acetylators
- Pyrazinamide and ethambutol — implied as part of standard four-drug TB regimen

**Phenotypes/diseases mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Tuberculosis (TB) — the primary disease context for all pharmacogenetic analysis
- Drug-induced hepatotoxicity (DIH) — all four cases of DIH occurred exclusively in NAT2 slow acetylators (SA phenotype); lower AcINH plasma levels were significantly associated with DIH occurrence
- Sputum culture conversion (SCC) / treatment response — intermediate acetylators (IA) showed faster time to sputum culture conversion (tSCC) than slow acetylators (by approximately 12.7 days, p=0.03), though this did not reach significance in multivariate analysis
- Early treatment failure — referenced from prior literature as linked to NAT2 genotype-guided dosing

---

### 3. PMC11144121.md
**Title:** Pharmacokinetic and neuroimmune pharmacogenetic impacts on slow-release morphine cancer pain control and adverse effects

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper investigates polymorphisms in opioid neuroimmunopharmacology pathway genes (TLR2, IL2, BDNF, IL6R, IL6) and their impact on morphine pharmacokinetics and adverse effects in cancer patients. NAT2 is not mentioned at any point. No acetylation-related content is present.

---

### 4. PMC11668066.md
**Title:** Associations Between the Polymorphisms in the Coding Sequence of SLCO1B1 and Blood Lipid Levels Before and After Treatment by Atorvastatin in the Chinese Han Adults with Dyslipidemia

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses entirely on SLCO1B1 gene polymorphisms and their influence on atorvastatin pharmacotherapy in dyslipidemia. NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere. No acetylator-related content is present.

---

### 5. PMC1401654.md
**Title:** Further evidence for an acetylator phenotype difference in the metabolism of hydralazine in man

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** BORDERLINE — The paper is directly about the NAT2 acetylator phenotype (slow vs. rapid acetylators) and its impact on hydralazine metabolism. However, this is a 1981 scanned-image document (full text not available as searchable text). The paper predates the NAT2 star allele nomenclature system; it characterizes the slow and rapid acetylator phenotypes by urinary metabolite ratios, not by specific gene variants. No specific NAT2 star alleles (*4, *5, *6, *7, *16) are named in the accessible abstract or metadata.

**Assessment:** The paper deals with the biological phenomenon underlying NAT2*4 (rapid) vs. NAT2*5/*6/*7/*16 (slow) distinctions — namely, the acetylator phenotype — but does not explicitly reference those alleles by name. It is phenotypically relevant but not specifically variant-designated.

**Drugs mentioned in association with acetylator phenotype:**
- Hydralazine (antihypertensive) — the paper directly studies the metabolite profile of hydralazine in slow vs. rapid acetylators; slow acetylators excreted less N-acetylhydrazinophthalazinone (NAcHPZ) and triazolphthalazine (TP) but more phthalazinone (PZ) and hydrazones (HH)

**Phenotypes/diseases mentioned in association with acetylator phenotype:**
- Hypertension — patients were hypertensive patients on hydralazine 100 mg twice daily
- Differential hydralazine metabolism — slow acetylators are implied to be at different pharmacokinetic and potentially toxicological risk

---

### 6. PMC1762324.md
**Title:** A Proof-Of-Principle Study of Epigenetic Therapy Added to Neoadjuvant Doxorubicin Cyclophosphamide for Locally Advanced Breast Cancer

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** BORDERLINE — This paper uses acetylator phenotype (slow vs. rapid acetylators) to dose-adjust hydralazine in breast cancer patients, employing sulfamethazine as a phenotyping probe. NAT2 is not explicitly named, and no specific star alleles are cited. Acetylator phenotype is used clinically (phenotyped, not genotyped) to guide hydralazine dosing: 182 mg/day for rapid acetylators and 83 mg/day for slow acetylators.

**Assessment:** Phenotypically relevant to NAT2 function (the paper is about acetylator-phenotype-guided drug dosing), but no NAT2 star alleles (*4, *5, *6, *7, *16) are named.

**Drugs mentioned in association with acetylator phenotype:**
- Hydralazine (used as a DNA demethylating agent, not as an antihypertensive) — dose-adjusted by acetylator status
- Magnesium valproate (HDAC inhibitor) — co-administered; not dose-adjusted by acetylator status
- Doxorubicin — neoadjuvant chemotherapy
- Cyclophosphamide — neoadjuvant chemotherapy
- Sulfamethazine — used as a phenotyping probe to classify acetylator status before treatment

**Phenotypes/diseases mentioned in association with acetylator phenotype:**
- Locally advanced breast cancer — the primary disease context
- Differential hydralazine plasma levels between slow and rapid acetylators (mean 299 vs. 246 ng/mL, not statistically significant after dose adjustment)
- DNA demethylation — hydralazine dose was adjusted to achieve equivalent demethylating effects across acetylator phenotypes

---

### 7. PMC2916058.md
**Title:** Polymorphisms of caffeine metabolism and estrogen receptor genes and risk of Parkinson's disease in men and women

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** YES — The paper explicitly genotypes three NAT2 SNPs: rs1801280 (Ile114Thr, defining NAT2*5 alleles), rs1799930 (Arg197Gln, defining NAT2*6 alleles), and rs1799931 (Gly286Gln, defining NAT2*7 alleles). These three SNPs are the canonical markers used to classify slow acetylator alleles within the NAT2*5, *6, and *7 groups. NAT2*4 is the wild-type/rapid allele (absence of these mutations). The paper does not use star allele nomenclature explicitly, but the SNPs it genotypes correspond directly to the defining mutations of NAT2*5, *6, and *7.

**Drugs mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Caffeine — the paper examines whether NAT2 slow vs. fast acetylator status modifies the association between caffeine intake and Parkinson's disease risk (no significant interaction was found)
- Hormone replacement therapy / post-menopausal hormones (PMH) — examined as an interaction variable with NAT2 status (no significant interaction found)

**Phenotypes/diseases mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Parkinson's disease (PD) — the primary outcome; the NAT2 slow acetylator phenotype was NOT significantly associated with altered risk of PD in this study (no association found for any of the three individual NAT2 SNPs or the combined slow acetylator phenotype)
- Familial Parkinson's disease — cited in references as having been previously linked to slow acetylator NAT2 genotype in other studies (Bandmann et al., 1997)

---

### 8. PMC3773276.md
**Title:** The CYP2C19*17 Variant is not Independently Associated with Clopidogrel Response

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses entirely on CYP2C19 variants (*2 and *17) and their effect on clopidogrel response (platelet aggregation, prodrug and active metabolite levels). NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere.

---

### 9. PMC3865618.md
**Title:** Association of Genetic Polymorphisms with Warfarin Dose Requirements in Chinese Patients

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper investigates SNPs in CYP4F2, CYP2C19, APOE, CALU, EPHX1, PROC, CYP2C9, and GGCX genes and their effect on warfarin dosing. NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere.

---

### 10. PMC5412025.md
**Title:** Tell-Tale SNPs: The Role of CYP2B6 in Methadone Fatalities

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses on CYP2B6 SNPs and their association with methadone-only fatalities. NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere.

---

### 11. PMC6486881.md
**Title:** Replication of the pharmacogenetic effect of rs678849 on buprenorphine efficacy in African-Americans with opioid use disorder

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses on the OPRD1 (opioid receptor delta 1) variant rs678849 and its effect on buprenorphine and methadone treatment outcomes in opioid use disorder. NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere.

---

### 12. PMC6813860.md
**Title:** Effect of N-Acetyltransferase 2 (NAT2) Genotype on the Pharmacokinetics of Hydralazine during Pregnancy

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** YES — This paper is the most explicitly variant-specific NAT2 paper in the set. It directly genotypes subjects using four NAT2 SNPs (rs181280/c.341T>C, rs1799930/c.590G>A, rs1799931/c.857G>A, rs1801279/c.191G>A) and reports each subject's exact star allele diplotype.

**Specific NAT2 alleles mentioned:**
- NAT2*4 — the fully functional rapid allele; rapid acetylators (RA) carried one *4 allele plus one slow allele
- NAT2*5 — slow allele; observed in combinations *4/*5, *5/*5, *5/*6, *5/*7
- NAT2*6 — slow allele; observed in combinations *4/*6, *5/*6, *6/*6
- NAT2*7 — slow allele; observed in combinations *4/*7, *5/*7
- NAT2*14 — also mentioned as a reduced-activity allele used for slow acetylator classification (though not observed in this specific cohort's listed genotypes)

**Rapid acetylators (RA, n=6) had NAT2 genotypes:** *4/*5 (n=2), *4/*6 (n=3), *4/*7 (n=1)
**Slow acetylators (SA, n=6) had NAT2 genotypes:** *5/*5 (n=1), *5/*6 (n=2), *5/*7 (n=1), *6/*6 (n=2)

**Drugs mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Hydralazine (oral antihypertensive) — the primary study drug; NAT2 genotype strongly determines hydralazine pharmacokinetics:
  - Rapid acetylators had ~3.5x faster apparent oral clearance (70.0 vs. 20.1 L/h)
  - Rapid acetylators had lower dose-normalized AUC (1.5 vs. 5.9 ng·h/mL)
  - Rapid acetylators had lower peak concentrations (0.77 vs. 4.04 ng/mL)
  - Rapid acetylators had larger volume of distribution (302 vs. 116 L/kg)
  - The MTP/hydralazine AUC ratio was ~10-fold higher in rapid acetylators (78 vs. 8)
- 3-methyl-1,2,4-triazolo[3,4-a]phthalazine (MTP) — the primary N-acetylation metabolite of hydralazine via NAT2
- Atenolol — co-prescribed antihypertensive (no significant interaction with hydralazine PK noted)

**Phenotypes/diseases mentioned in association with these variants:**
- Hypertension during pregnancy — the clinical indication for hydralazine; all subjects were pregnant women with hypertension receiving oral hydralazine
- NAT2-genotype-dependent pharmacokinetics — the paper establishes that NAT2*4 (rapid allele) versus NAT2*5/*6/*7 (slow alleles) drives significant differences in hydralazine exposure during pregnancy; no gestational age-dependent effect was observed independently of NAT2 phenotype

---

### 13. PMC9582748.md
**Title:** Effects of CYP2C19 genetic polymorphisms on the cure rates of H. pylori in patients treated with the proton pump inhibitors: An updated meta-analysis

**Relevant to NAT2*4, *5, *6, *7, or *16?** NO

This paper focuses entirely on CYP2C19 polymorphisms and their effect on Helicobacter pylori eradication rates using proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, rabeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole). NAT2 is not mentioned anywhere.

---
